question: Does the elimination of large-bore RCS piping adequately address LOCA concerns?

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, outlines the required valve testing procedures, and specifies that an in-situ DHRS heat removal test will be conducted on the first installed reactor module as part of the initial test program.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the types of steel and nickel-based alloys used, their protective claddings, and compliance with relevant BPVC material specifications to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until its source is confirmed, with leakage rates determined by RCS inventory balance and compared to containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates; the detection systems are reliable and support leak-before-break analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is responsible for removing heat from the reactor core and transferring it to the steam generators using natural circulation, driven by density and elevation differences. The system includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and various valves, and features mechanisms for removing noncondensible gases during both normal and emergency operations to maintain system integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, ensuring safe cooling without external power during non-LOCA events. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, preventing fuel or pressure boundary limits from being exceeded, and operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), though both can function together if needed. The DHRS's reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs, and it meets relevant regulatory and design criteria.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System piping, specifically focusing on steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the main degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear remaining the primary concern and corrosion mechanisms assessed as low risk due to slow defect growth rates.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within regulatory safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to prevent fuel design limit violations during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It details how the steam generators are integrated into the reactor vessel, forming part of the primary and secondary pressure boundaries, and references other sections for further information on specific components.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: This section describes the design features of each NuScale Power Module (NPM) that ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life, in accordance with regulatory definitions. It details the components and systems included in the RCPB, such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment isolation valves, safety valves, and associated piping, emphasizing their roles in maintaining pressure containment and system safety.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines that preservice and inservice inspections and testing of pressure-retaining components are conducted according to the latest applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements, as mandated by federal regulations, with inspection intervals and code editions specified in the inspection program.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: This section describes the evaluation of the NuScale Power Plant’s system performance, focusing on optimizing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressure to maximize electrical generation and maintain safety margins. Calculations, verified by hand and using NRELAP5 software, account for uncertainties and ensure low pressure losses to support the plant’s natural circulation design. The analysis also notes that uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains two integral steam generators (SGs) within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which also houses other key components such as the pressurizer and safety valves. The steam generator system (SGS) includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the RPV itself forming the SG shell and pressure boundary. The SG tubes and associated welds are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, ensuring separation between the primary and secondary sides.

edges.description: Section B provides material specifications and component descriptions for parts of the Reactor Coolant System that are subject to the inspection and testing requirements outlined in Section A.

Section A describes inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B discusses the challenges of detecting RCS pressure boundary leakage, both addressing aspects of system integrity and leakage management relevant to LOCA concerns.

Section A details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B describes the RCS's venting features and pressure boundary integrity, both addressing system features that contribute to LOCA mitigation by ensuring component reliability and venting capability.

Section A describes inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the system described in Section B as providing decay heat removal for non-LOCA events, connecting them through the design and operational role of the DHRS.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B addresses the integrity and risk assessment of SG tubing in the reactor coolant system; both sections provide evidence supporting the adequacy of design features that mitigate LOCA concerns by ensuring component reliability and compliance with established standards.

Section A provides detailed design information about the pressurizer and its materials, which are part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary discussed in Section B's context of leakage detection and integrity.

Section A references material specifications and component descriptions that are further detailed in other sections, while Section B discusses operational experience and risk considerations for reactor coolant system piping, with both sections providing information relevant to the adequacy of LOCA mitigation by design and material choices.

Section A discusses the challenges in detecting RCS boundary leakage due to the absence of traditional components and instrumentation limitations, while Section B describes the RCS system's design features that maintain RCPB integrity, connecting them through the topic of how system design and detection capabilities address LOCA concerns.

Section A discusses the inability to distinguish RCS leakage sources due to instrumentation limitations, while Section B describes the DHRS's role in removing decay heat during non-LOCA events, indicating that the DHRS is not specifically designed for LOCA scenarios addressed by Section A.

Section A describes the RCS system's features that maintain pressure boundary integrity and facilitate gas removal, while Section B outlines the DHRS's role in removing decay heat during non-LOCA events, connecting them through their complementary safety functions but not directly addressing LOCA concerns.

Section A describes the RCS design features that maintain pressure boundary integrity and facilitate gas removal, while Section B discusses the design and operational experience of RCS piping (specifically SG tubing), both providing information relevant to evaluating whether the elimination of large-bore RCS piping addresses LOCA concerns by focusing on system integrity and failure modes.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's role in non-LOCA events, while Section B discusses the integrity and risk profile of RCS piping, with both sections connected through their implications for LOCA risk management and system reliability.

Section A describes the elimination of external RCS piping in the NuScale design, while Section B details inspection requirements for remaining system components, indicating that although large-bore piping is eliminated, regulatory oversight continues for other relevant components.
